title: Preparing multiplexed 16S rRNA gene amplicons (with fusion primers) for the Illumina MiSeq
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"a9mq4","text":"The following protocol is for the generation of paired-end sequencing reads of 16S rRNA gene (V4 or V4V5) amplicons with dual barcodes (i.e.: “indexes”) on the Illumina MiSeq machine using v3 600 cycle chemistry. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":174,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2v8mr","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4j9ga","text":"We use this protocol to make MiSeq libraries from DNA extracted from a variety of environmental samples, including seawater, freshwater, and swabs from hosts or surfaces of interest. This protocol is modified from dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.4r3l277k3g1y/v1 and makes use of 'fusion' primers or PCR primers that include not only the primer sequence, but also the Illumina adapter and Nextera index. This allows samples to be indexed for amplicon sequencing using a single PCR rather than using a two-step PCR approach described here.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":214,"length":48},{"key":1,"offset":533,"length":4}],"data":{}},{"key":"c18et","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"28rkt","text":"Many thanks to André Comeau and the Integrated Microbiome Resource at Dalhousie University for so clearly describing methods and allowing for reproducibility. The resources provided here https://github.com/LangilleLab/microbiome_helper/wiki and in their publication https://journals.asm.org/doi/10.1128/msystems.00127-16 were instrumental in developing our in-house protocols.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":2,"offset":187,"length":53},{"key":3,"offset":266,"length":54}],"data":{}},{"key":"df76s","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"as86o","text":"Note: This protocol leverages combinatorial dual indexes. For other Illumina instruments (e.g. NextSeq), unique dual indexes may improve data quality and reduce index hopping. For a bit more information about the difference between unique dual indexes and combinatorial dual indexes, check out this resource.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":4,"offset":294,"length":13}],"data":{}},{"key":"borjr","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cug6","text":"If you have any questions, please don't hesitate to contact us!","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"4A46B5F36BBB11EF89A50A58A9FEAC02","url":"https://dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.4r3l277k3g1y/v1"}},"1":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"D054E5DD1DF4470AA7BDE3A41D2F520F","url":"https://support.illumina.com/documents/documentation/chemistry_documentation/16s/16s-metagenomic-library-prep-guide-15044223-b.pdf"}},"2":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"F5AB55296EEA11EF969A0A58A9FEAC02","url":"https://github.com/LangilleLab/microbiome_helper/wiki"}},"3":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"FCD5B00E6EEA11EF911D0A58A9FEAC02","url":"https://journals.asm.org/doi/10.1128/msystems.00127-16"}},"4":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"D1BCE36CD77E4122B8401CC629F6C9D3","url":"https://knowledge.illumina.com/library-preparation/general/library-preparation-general-reference_material-list/000002344"}}}}

der/catalog/product/12331D"}},"3":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"E1A25251F0C6444E949442F94BAC7300","url":"https://www.froggabio.com/stf-a120-group.html"}},"4":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"E6CCF2E4A6BE48FD8940E58F4B278687","url":"https://www.eppendorf.com/ca-en/Products/Liquid-Handling/Pipette-Tips/epDualfilterTIPS-p-PF-9246"}},"5":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"7F7B929B11534889B25A089226BF5272","url":"https://ecatalog.corning.com/life-sciences/b2b/CA/en/Media,-Sera,-and-Reagents/High-Quality-Water/Molecular-Biology-Grade-Water/Corning%C2%AE-Molecular-Biology-Grade-Water/p/46-000-CI"}},"6":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"E0C74EDF6B7644D29D156D84DA615051","url":"https://www.sigmaaldrich.com/CA/en/product/sigma/ep022510509"}},"7":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"5EA889FFC83143C0BAE62371D4C47037","url":"https://www.sigmaaldrich.com/CA/en/product/sigma/hs29025g"}},"8":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"1C21D33599864EABB4372C3AB6CBEA38","url":"https://www.sigmaaldrich.com/CA/en/product/sigma/r6901"}},"9":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"5121152CE00F46A5805811BE985EC632","url":"https://www.sigmaaldrich.com/CA/en/product/sigma/baf188460020"}}}}
steps: Let's get started: On ice or in a cooler block, prepare the following PCR master mix for sample PCR Plate 1 in a 5 mL Eppendorf tubes. Primer plates were prepared using the primer format described here Preparing Indexed Primer Plates (IDT Ultramers) for the Illumina MiSeq - Nextera Dual Indices . This protocol assumes that both your template DNA and fusion PCR primers have already been aliquoted into 96-well plates. This allows for use of multichannel pipettes and for working in a more high-throughput format. Samples are amplified in duplicate, so if you feel so compelled, prepare enough master mix for 2 PCR plates (x210 rxns column below). Reagent 25 ul rxn x105 rxns x210 rxns 1 PCR-grade Water 5.5 µL 578 μL 1155 μL 2 2x Taq FroggaMix 12.5 µL 1313 μL 2625 μL 3 BSA (10 mg/ml) 1 µL 105 µL 210 μL 4 F primer (2.5 µM) 2 µL added after added after 5 R primer (2.5 µM) 2 µL added after added after 6 Template (sample DNA) 2 µL added after added after Reagent Details: Taq FroggaMix Froggabio Catalog #FBTAQM96 Recombinant Albumin, Molecular Biology Grade New England Biolabs Catalog #B9200S We have recently switched away from BSA to Recombinant Albumin, because traditional molecular biology grade BSA was resulting in amplification in PCRs for other genes (namely 12S-MiFish). We dilute to 10 mg/mL before use. Corning® 100 mL Molecular Biology Grade Water Tested to USP Sterile Purified Water Specifications Corning Catalog #46-000-CI Primer Details: The actual oligo/ fusion primer sequences used are much longer and described in Preparing Indexed Primer Plates (IDT Ultramers) for the Illumina MiSeq - Nextera Dual Indices but the specific primers, that are embedded in the fusion primer are listed below, just for reference. Please do not use these shorter primers in this protocol! We do use the shorter 'normal' primers to test how well the samples will amplify or troubleshoot samples that are tricky to amplify before using the more expensive fusion primers to prepare the library. Also, don't hesitate to reach out if you have any questions! Primer Name Sequence (5'-3') Target Gene Region Publication 1 515F-Y GTGYCAGCMGCCGCGGTAA 16S-V4; 16S-V4V5 Parada et al. 2016; https://doi.org/10.1111/1462-2920.13023 2 806R GGACTACNVGGGTWTCTAAT 16S-V4 Apprill et al. 2015, https://doi.org/10.3354/ame01753 3 926R CCGYCAATTYMTTTRAGTTT 16S-V4V5 Parada et al. 2016; https://doi.org/10.1111/1462-2920.13023
Pipet 125 µL of the master mix into each of the 8 wells of 4 sets of strip tubes. From these strip tubes, you will transfer the master mix into the PCR plate (s) using a multichannel pipette (MCP).

We are transitioning away from using traditional gel verification of PCR success to using our Qiaxcel System for this step but given that not all labs would have access to this system or something similar (e.g. Agilent Tapestation), we describe the more 'traditional' gel verification method here. We typically run completed PCRs on a 2% agarose gel to verify their success (or mourn their failure). To optimize time, make your gel while the PCRs are running. We have a BioRad Sub-Cell Model 192 Cell Electrophoresis system (among others). Sadly this is now discontinued but it allows you to run 2 PCR plates of samples simultaneously with the 25 x 10 cm and 25 x 15 cm trays. Weigh out 3.6 g of Certified Molecular Biology Agarose Bio-Rad Laboratories Catalog #1613101 into a large Erlenmeyer flask (e.g. 500 mL). Add 180 mL of 1X TBE buffer (we make a 1X stock from 10X TBE Buffer Bio-Rad Laboratories Catalog #1610770 ) and swirl to mix.

Clean and concentrate the remaining ~ 45 µL of Aggregated PCR Plate 1 . Preparations Prepare the purification in the post-PCR working space. Size selection can be achieved using different ratios of magnetic beads to sample. A ratio of bead to a sample of 0.8-1.5 will efficiently purify the amplicons away from primer dimers and allow the selection of fragments larger than 200 bp. Materials Sera-Mag SPRI beads. We are currently using Sera-Mag SpeedBeads Carboxylate-Modified Magnetic Particles GE Healthcare Catalog #44152105050350 If not already prepared follow this protocol. Serapure Beads Preparation and Testing Magnetic 96-well plate stand (for example: https://www.thermofisher.com/order/catalog/product/12331D ) Anhydrous Ethanol to make a fresh 80% ethanol solution Molecular grade water UV for 30 minutes the following: 96-well PCR plates (or 8-strip tubes) Sharpie Pipette tips Multichannel pipettes Pipettes Sterile Nuclease-Free Water Remove the magnetic beads from the fridge (allow 30 min to reach room temperature).

Pipet 19 µL of master mix from the master mix strip tubes into each well of the PCR plate (which is held in a PCR cooler plate to keep all reagents cold throughout this process), one column at a time with the MCP. You can use the same set of 8 tips to aliquot your master mix into all columns of your PCR plate.

Uncover the DNA Template Plate 1 , align it along the top (or beside, depending on your preference!) of PCR Plate 1 (which now contains master mix, forward primers and reverse primers) and dispense 2 µL of template DNA into each corresponding well, one column at a time using the MCP. Seal the PCR Plate 1 with PCR film and either keep on ice until both PCR plates are ready for the thermal cyclers or place in the thermal cycler right away, as per below. Remember to change tips after every column.

Uncover one column of the Reverse Set 1 Primer Plate (R1) , align it horizontally on the bench to the left of your PCR Plate 1 and and dispense 2 µL into each well, one column at a time, using the MCP. In the reverse primer plates, each column contains the same index. Remember to change your pipet tips after each column to prevent cross contamination.

Remove the protective film from column 1 of the Forward Set 1 Primer Plate (F1) , align it horizontally on the bench to the left of PCR Plate 1 and pipet 2 µL into from each well of column 1 of Primer Plate F1 into column 1 of your PCR Plate using the MCP. Now, remove more of the film (or caps, if not using a sealing film) from the next column ( column 2 ) of Primer Plate F1 and pipet 2 µL into column 2 of your PCR plate . Do this one column at a time using the MCP until you have aliquoted contents from each column of your primer plate into the corresponding column of your PCR plate. In the forward primer plates, each row contains a different index. Remember to change your pipet tips after each column to prevent cross contamination.

Once PCR setup is complete, seal the plates with PCR film, place in thermal cyclers (we have BioRad T100 Thermal Cyclers) and run the following program for a total of 30 total cycles : A B C 1 Initial denaturation 94°C 3 min 2 Denaturation 94°C 45 s 3 Annealing 50°C 60 s 4 Extension 72°C 90 s 5 GO TO Step 2 29 times 6 Final Extension 72°C 10 min 7 Hold 4°C forever

Once the two PCRs for DNA Plate 1, 2 and 3 are complete, repeat steps 2-10 to prepare PCR Plates for DNA Template Plate 4 using Forward Set 1 Primer Plate (F2) and Reverse Set 2 Primer Plate (R2). We typically only multiplex 3 plates of samples for a MiSeq Run, but with the available index combinations (F1R1, F1R2, F2R1, F2R2), it is possible to multiplex 4.
Once the two PCRs for Plate 1 are complete, repeat steps 2-10 to prepare PCR Plates for DNA Template Plate 2 using Forward Set 1 Primer Plate (F1) and Reverse Set 2 Primer Plate (R2).
Once the two PCRs for DNA Plate 1 and 2 are complete, repeat steps 2-10 to prepare PCR Plates for DNA Template Plate 3 using Forward Set 1 Primer Plate (F2) and Reverse Set 2 Primer Plate (R1).
Repeat steps 14-24 for PCR Plates 2.
Repeat steps 14-24 for PCR Plates 3.
If you are intending to multiplex 4 plates of samples, repeat steps 14-24 for PCR Plates 4.
Repeat steps 29-38 for Aggregated PCR Plate 2 .
Repeat steps 29-38 for Aggregated PCR Plate 4 .
Repeat steps 29-38 for Aggregated PCR Plate 3 .

Day-of Preparation: Several hours before getting started, make sure you have a couple PCR cooler plates and cool racks for larger tubes in the freezer. These will be used to keep your reagents cool during the PCR setup. We set up PCRs in a clean room, in a PCR workstation. Before getting started, UV for 00:30:00 the following: 96-well PCR plates 8-well strip tubes 5 mL and 1.5 mL eppendorf tubes Sharpie Pipette tips Multichannel pipettes Pipettes Sterile Nuclease-Free Water Any necessary racks for holding tubes Do NOT UV your DNA template plates or your PCR reagents!
Repeat steps 4 - 8 for your replicate PCR plate, if preparing it at the same time (recommended since everything is thawed!).
Microwave for 00:02:00 or until boiling. With a protective glove on, swirl flask. Be careful when you swirl the flask as some steam will likely come out of the flask.
Microwave for another 00:01:00 and allow the solution to boil until it is clear, and the agarose is completely dissolved.
Using a protective glove, remove the flask with melted agarose solution from the microwave and allow to cool slightly.
Assemble gel tray in gel caster.

When flask has cooled enough that you can pick it up without it being too hot, add 9 µL of RedSafe Nucleic Acid Staining Solution Froggabio Catalog #21141 to the flask and gently swirl to mix. Now you can gently pour the liquid agarose solution into the gel tray. Add 4 x 51-well combs to the gel tray.
Allow gel to cool and set completely, for at least 00:30:00 .

Once the PCRs are complete and the gel is completely set, remove the gel from the gel caster and place it in the gel electrophoresis box. Add 1x TBE until it completely covers the gel with a couple centimeters of liquid above the gel. Remove the combs.

Remove the PCR plates from the thermocycler and briefly spin the plate to remove any liquid from the foil. Gently pull back the seal, and load 2 µL (or up to 5 ul) of each sample into gel wells using a MCP. Our PCR master mix from FroggaBio already has loading dye in it, so we can load the PCR product directly to the wells, without first mixing an aliquot with any sort of loading dye. Also, with our set-up and the 51-well combs, the MCP loads samples into every other well. So you load column 1 of your PCR plate into the gel with the samples being loaded into every other well. Then you load column 2, where column 2A will be loaded right next to column 1A in the gel.

After checking that your samples amplified successfully on your Gel Visualization system (e.g. BioRad XR Gel Documentation System ) pool the replicate PCR plates (make sure the PCR plates are oriented the same way before doing this!) using a MCP. Add the contents of column 1 from one PCR replicate plate to the contents of column 1 in the other replicate plate. Continue this process until every column and sample has been pooled. This is now your aggregate PCR plate.

Vortex the beads before use. Add 36 μl beads to 45 μl of PCR product to obtain a ratio of 0.8. Pipette up and down ten times (or until the solution is well mixed – you will see that the color changes). Spin tubes down to remove drops from the walls.
Incubate at room temperature without shaking for 00:05:00 . Then, place the plate on the magnetic stand until the supernatant has cleared (~ 00:03:00 ).
Remove the supernatant with a MCP, ensuring to not disturb the beads.
With the samples on the magnetic rack, wash the beads by adding 180 μl of freshly prepared 80% ethanol and incubate for 30s. Carefully remove the supernatant without disturbing the beads.
Repeat the washing step. go to step #32

Remove all residual ethanol using a pipette and air dry, leaving the samples on the magnetic stand (~ 00:05:00 ). The drying time depends on the type of the magnetic rack – the O-ring magnet dries faster than the side magnet. Keep an eye on the beads and do not over-dry. Otherwise, you will not get an efficient DNA recovery.
Remove the plate from the magnetic stand and add 40 μl of nuclease-free water for elution. Gently pipet up and down ten times to resuspend the beads. Incubate the plate at room temperature for 5 min.
Place the plate back on the magnetic rack for at least 5 min or until the supernatant is cleared.
Carefully transfer 30 μl of the clear supernatant to a new plate. Seal the plate.

Name the plate. For example: Project, Gene_name + Primer Plate Combo Used, Cleaned PCRs, Date, Initials. Samples can be stored in the fridge for the short term (e.g. overnight before quantification), but should be placed in the freezer (-20 °C) for long-term storage.

Use a fluorometric quantification method that uses dsDNA dyes to measure the concentration of your libraries. If doing a few samples, we use a Qubit. If quantifying an entire plate of samples (or more) we often use a microplate reader, like a BMG LabTech CLARIOstar. If using Qubit, give preference to the broad range kit if you visualize a strong band in the gel. For either method, follow manufacturers instructions for how to use the kit and the instrument (Qubit or plate reader). Qubit dsDNA BR (Broad Range) assay Thermo Fisher Scientific Catalog #Q32850 Quant-it™ PicoGreen® dsDNA Assay Kit Life Technologies Catalog #P7589

Calculate sample volume to have a final amount of 10-40 ng per sample. This amount may vary depending on the overall quantification. For example, if, on average, the concentration of your samples is about 3 ng/μl and you have 20 μl of product, you can add up to 60 ng of cleaned per sample (although it is ideal to aim for less than the max possible). You will want to pool approximately the same amount of cleaned, amplified DNA for each sample to have a more even read count among samples in the library.
Following manufacturers instructions, measure the final library pool concentration on Qubit using Qubit® dsDNA HS Assay Kit Thermo Fisher Scientific Catalog #Q32854
Label tube. For example: Project_Name-Gene_name, Pooled Amplicons. Date, Initials, pool concentration.
Library fragment size (BP) is determined using Bioanalyzer chips and reagents (DNA High Sensitivity kit) Agilent Technologies OR QIAxcel DNA Fast Analysis Kit Qiagen Catalog #929008
Molarity of the final amplicon library pool is assessed using NEBNext Library Quant Kit for Illumina - 100 rxns New England Biolabs Catalog #E7630S

The library is then sequenced, according the manufacturers instructions, on an Illumina MiSeq using MiSeq v3 Sequencing Reagents (600 cycles) Illumina, Inc. Catalog #MS-102-3003 with pair-end setup (2*300 bp), spiked with 10-15% PhiX Control v3 Illumina, Inc. Catalog #FC-110-3001

Make DNA Template Plates We aliquot sample DNA into 96-well plates to use for PCRs (called our DNA template plates ). For our samples, we have found that a concentration of 2.5 ng/ul typically yields successful amplification from our seawater samples (depths ranging from 0 m to 250 m; coastal setting). Some people prefer doing a 1:10 dilution of sample DNA rather than targeting a specific input concentration. All this to say, that for 16S rRNA PCRs from coastal seawater, some form of sample dilution is often needed, depending on the DNA extraction protocol used, how efficient that extraction was, and how deep the sample was collected from. In the process of creating these DNA template plates, we always leave well 12H empty (and/or add PCR-grade water to it) to ensure space for a PCR negative with each plate of samples to be amplified.
Run your gel at a voltage and for a duration of time appropriate for the size of gel you are running. For example, for a large 2% gel we might right at 100 V for 00:30:00

After determining amount of DNA to pool for each sample, it is time to pool the DNA for each sample! This takes a bit of time, so get comfortable. Check the final volume that you will get after pooling by adding all of the samples volumes you will pool – sometimes you will end up with 2 mL or more. Then use the proper Eppendorf tube for pooling (1.5, 2.0, or 5 mL).
creator: Colleen Kellogg
